title: DOM State, Events, and Accessible Interaction · Scenario Checkpoint
summary: Build a keyboard-operable service-status interface whose DOM, focus, announcements, and visual state stay synchronized. Every item uses a fresh case and misconception-specific distractors. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for an accessible interactive service-status interface. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Query, create, move, replace, and remove DOM nodes with explicit missing-element and ownership handling.
Derive DOM output from application state without duplicating conflicting sources of truth.
Trace event target, currentTarget, capture, bubble, composed paths, and cancellation before delegating behavior.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.javascript:
// javascript-basics cumulative stakeholder program.
// Add one runnable behavior, assertion, changed case, and evidence return per activity.

steps.title: Diagnose the hidden failure
steps.instruction:
quiz case — An accessible status filter has 4 options, works by keyboard and pointer, survives dynamic list updates through delegation, restores focus after removal, and announces the changed result count. Competency probe: Announce meaningful asynchronous changes without noisy live regions or focus theft.

The visible result looks correct, but its invariant or value contract is unverified. What must happen next?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for announce meaningful asynchronous changes without noisy live regions or focus theft.

content.text: An accessible status filter has 4 options, works by keyboard and pointer, survives dynamic list updates through delegation, restores focus after removal, and announces the changed result count. Competency probe: Announce meaningful asynchronous changes without noisy live regions or focus theft.

Competency probe: Announce meaningful asynchronous changes without noisy live regions or focus theft.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: Moving focus to every updated message is the most accessible announcement.

options.text: Run the Live Updates boundary case for an accessible interactive service-status interface, inspect the actual mechanism, then accept or repair the claim.
Accept the Live Updates result because visible an accessible interactive service-status interface output is the highest authority. This does not resolve diagnose the hidden failure.

For diagnose the hidden failure, apply Live Updates without its stated preconditions, omit the an accessible interactive service-status interface trace and counterexample, and treat one plausible output as complete proof. This does not satisfy Live Updates for an accessible interactive service-status interface.

steps.title: Defend one difficult decision
steps.instruction: Write the quiz evidence note for Live Updates: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — An accessible status filter has 7 options, works by keyboard and pointer, survives dynamic list updates through delegation, restores focus after removal, and announces the changed result count. Competency probe: Announce meaningful asynchronous changes without noisy live regions or focus theft.
steps.why: Retrieving Live Updates through the concrete an accessible interactive service-status interface case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Announce meaningful asynchronous changes without noisy live regions or focus theft.

hints: Name the Live Updates precondition or invariant that the quiz case must preserve.
